- **Clear Runway to AWS:** only firewalls and a few ports to open, then we can point the robot at AWS.

**Next step:** point the robot at AWS and wire up the cloud pipeline.

**Speaker notes:** We are much closer to the cloud architecture we want. The "robot and internet at the same time" problem is solved and the AWS path is validated.

---

## Slide 7: Gazebo to Cloud to Real Robot (Shibo, Simulation)

*Layout: three flow cards with arrows, plus two info cards.*

The same bridge pattern connects simulation, the cloud, and the physical robot.

1. **Gazebo Simulation:** expose the Gazebo API for control.
2. **Framework on AWS:** plug the API into the framework, deployed in the cloud.
3. **Physical TurtleBot:** the same pattern links a deployed model to the real robot.

- Leveraging existing Nav2 navigation experience.
- *Open:* exact AWS bridge details still under investigation.

**Speaker notes:** Sim, cloud, and real robot all use the same connection pattern. One or two lines on the Gazebo work and what exposing its API unlocks.

---

## Slide 8: Three Pillars of Security (Fazheng)

*Layout: three numbered cards with priority tags, plus a note band.*

1. **Model I/O Guardrails (CRITICAL):** zero trust on models, validate both input and output.
2. **ROS Vulnerabilities (HIGH):** harden the robot's own software attack surface.
3. **WiFi Test Environment (MEDIUM):** man-in-the-middle risk during testing over WiFi.

Expanded guardrails research file posted to the Teams chat.

**Speaker notes:** Lead with the guardrails point. We do not trust model output by default and we validate both input and output. The other two pillars are the ROS attack surface and the WiFi test environment.

---

## Slide 9: Accessible by Design (Sibgha)

*Layout: quote callout, three example cards, plus a requirement band.*

> Robots in similar settings repeatedly fail on accessibility, even when everything else works.

- **Speak Without Speaking:** chat-based interaction for users who cannot speak.
- **Reach Every Space:** if a wheelchair user cannot access an area, the robot assists accordingly.
- **A Human to Reach:** a point person if someone gets stuck mid-route, plus determining and setting those points.

A Northeastern requirement for real deployment. User stories defined next, to set specific, measurable accessibility parameters.

**Speaker notes:** Accessibility is the differentiator and a deployment gate. A case study inspired this direction, and the paper will be shared with the team.

---

## Slide 10: UX Direction & Key Decisions

*Layout: left UX card, right three decision cards.*

**UX & App Direction**
- Start designing the user-facing interfaces and mobile app.
- App-architecture guidance from Jack flows to Kalhar, then built together.
- Get the core working end to end first, then layer features on top.

**Key Decisions**
- **Multilingual: deprioritized for now.** Not the primary goal; easy later, with many failure modes.
- **No live demo Wednesday.** Present grounded progress instead.
- **Build core first, expand later.** Stabilize the pipeline before adding features.

**Speaker notes:** Design work starts now because we will need it soon. These are deliberate scoping calls, not things we forgot.

---

## Slide 11: Next Steps by Owner (Roadmap)

*Layout: four owner cards, plus a references band.*

- **Kalhar:** point the robot at AWS and open required ports; build and consolidate the slide deck.
- **Shibo:** nail down the Gazebo API to AWS bridge; document the bridge approach for the team.
- **Fazheng:** expand the guardrails research; threat model for ROS and WiFi.
- **Sibgha:** research accessibility and draft user stories; start the mobile app and UI, share the case study.

*References to attach:* accessibility case-study paper (Sibgha), and the guardrails research file in Teams (Fazheng).

**Speaker notes:** Concrete, owned next steps so the panel sees momentum.

---

## Slide 12: Closing

*Layout: dark closing slide.*

**Off the bench. Onto campus.**
Cloud path validated. Four workstreams advancing in parallel.
**Questions?**
Kalhar Pandya, Sibgha Ahmad, Shibo Bai, Fazheng Han

**Speaker notes:** End on the milestone and the four-track structure, then invite questions.

# GuideMate — Weekly Update Presentation Notes

**Presentation date:** Wednesday, June 17, 2026
**Prepared from:** Team sync, June 15, 2026 (transcribed)
**Format:** Build these slides in Google Slides → review/edit text together → export to PPT
**Demo:** No live demo this Wednesday — goal is a *grounded* progress update.

> **How to use this file:** Each slide below has the on-slide bullets plus *speaker notes*.
> Everyone should refine the points in their own area before we consolidate. Kalhar will
> drop these into Google Slides slide-by-slide; the team edits text, then we generate the PPT.

> ⚠️ **Verify owners:** topic→person mapping was inferred from a rough auto-transcript.
> Correct any name before presenting.

---

## Slide 1 — Title

**GuideMate — TurtleBot 4 Guide Robot**
Weekly Progress Update · CS7980 Capstone · June 17, 2026

Team: Kalhar Pandya · Sibgha Ahmad · Shibo Bai · Fazheng Han

*Speaker notes:* Quick framing — this week we crossed a real infrastructure milestone and locked in our four workstreams (connectivity/cloud, simulation, security, accessibility/policy).

---

## Slide 2 — Agenda & Workstreams

- **Connectivity & Cloud** — Kalhar
- **Simulation & Open-Source Framework** — Shibo
- **Cybersecurity & Guardrails** — Fazheng
- **Accessibility & Policy (+ UX)** — Sibgha
- Decisions · Roadmap · Next steps

*Speaker notes:* Each of us owns one area and contributes our own slides. We'll keep this update tight and evidence-based.

---

## Slide 3 — Project Recap

- **GuideMate**: an AI-assisted guide robot built on **TurtleBot 4** (Raspberry Pi 4 + iRobot Create 3 base, ROS 2 Humble).
- Two units in the lab: `turtlebot468` and `turtlebot436`.
- Direction (post-discussion with Jack): make the robot **accessible**, **deployable**, and **secure** — not just functional in the lab.

*Speaker notes:* Reminder of what GuideMate is and the shift toward real-world deployability. Accessibility is being framed as a Northeastern requirement for taking this further.

---

## Slide 4 — Where We Were: The Bottleneck

- Until now the robot was effectively **stuck in the lab**.
- Hard constraint: you could use **the robot OR the internet — not both** at the same time.
- Everything had to be typed in manually; no remote development.

*Speaker notes:* This sets up why this week's networking result matters. The "robot vs. internet" trade-off was our biggest day-to-day blocker.

---

## Slide 5 — Connectivity Breakthrough (Kalhar)

- Set up **Gazebo + Linux** locally; ran the expected lab experiments.
- Got the robot running on **NUwave (campus-wide WiFi)** — it can now operate **across campus**, not just in the lab.
- Ran **three Claude Code agents simultaneously** — one on the laptop, one on the Alienware, one on the TurtleBot itself — all **communicating over the network** and negotiating network protocols.
- Sent **commands device-to-device over NUwave** (one laptop → another / → robot) and the robot followed them.

*Speaker notes:* The headline: the robot is no longer confined to the lab. The multi-agent setup proves devices can coordinate over the campus network. This is a lasting capability the lab benefits from going forward.

---

## Slide 6 — Remote Access & Cloud Readiness (Kalhar)

- **Remote access from home** via the **Northeastern VPN** — develop from anywhere.
- Remote setup also live on the lab **Alienware** (kept at the university).
- Validated **AWS IoT Core** connectivity, **time servers**, and general network reachability.
- Finding: nothing is fundamentally blocked — it was **firewalls + a few protocols/ports** to open. **Ready to point the robot at AWS.**

*Speaker notes:* We're now much closer to the cloud architecture we want. "Robot + internet at the same time" is solved, and the AWS path is validated, so the next step is wiring the robot to AWS.

---

## Slide 7 — Simulation & Open-Source Framework (Shibo)

- How **open-source robot frameworks** work and how we **build on top** of them.
- Plan to **expose the Gazebo API** and plug it into our framework, **deployed on AWS**.
- The **same bridge pattern** connects a model deployed on AWS to the **physical TurtleBot**.
- Drawing on existing **Nav2 navigation** experience (footprint/navigation stack) for our architecture.
- *Open:* exact AWS bridge details still under investigation.

*Speaker notes:* Sim-to-cloud-to-real all use the same connection pattern. One or two lines on the Gazebo work you've done and what exposing its API unlocks.

---

## Slide 8 — Cybersecurity & Guardrails (Fazheng)

Three pillars (from the framework shared two weeks ago):

1. **Model I/O guardrails — "zero trust on models, including their output."** *(Most important.)*
2. Security **vulnerabilities in ROS** itself.
3. Security in **robot testing over WiFi** — e.g. **man-in-the-middle** risk during testing.

- Expanded research file on model guardrails **posted to the Teams chat** (earlier version was too short).

*Speaker notes:* Lead with the guardrails point — we don't trust model output by default and validate both input and output. The other two are the ROS attack surface and the WiFi/test-environment threat model.

---

## Slide 9 — Accessibility & Policy (Sibgha)

- Focus narrowed to **accessibility** — case studies show robots in similar settings repeatedly **fail on accessibility**, even when everything else works.
- Maximize accessibility for **all potential users**, e.g.:
  - **Chat-based interaction** for users who can't speak.
  - If someone is in a **wheelchair** and cannot access a certain area, the robot should **assist accordingly**.
  - A human **point person** users can reach if they get stuck mid-route — **determining and setting those points**.
- Treated as a **Northeastern requirement** for real deployment.
- **User stories** to be defined in the coming weeks so we can create **specific parameters of accessibility**.

*Speaker notes:* Accessibility is the differentiator and a deployment gate. A case study inspired this direction — paper to be shared with the team / added to references.

---

## Slide 10 — UX & App Direction (Sibgha)

- Begin designing the **user-facing interfaces** and **mobile app** (layout, options offered).
- App-architecture recommendations from **Jack** to flow to Kalhar, then build together.
- Sequencing: **get the core working end-to-end first**, then layer features on top.

*Speaker notes:* Design work starts now because we'll need it soon. Architecture is coordinated with the robot side so the app and robot fit together.

---

## Slide 11 — Key Decisions

- **Multilingual support: deprioritized for now.** Not the primary goal; trivial later (prompt + LLM choice) but has many failure modes. Revisit after the core works.
- **No live demo Wednesday** — present grounded progress instead.
- **Build first, expand later** — stabilize the core pipeline, then add accessibility/UX features.

*Speaker notes:* Be clear these are deliberate scoping calls, not things we forgot.

---

## Slide 12 — Roadmap / Next Steps

| Owner | Next step |
|---|---|
| Kalhar | Point the robot at **AWS**; consolidate slides; open required firewalls/ports |
| Shibo | Nail down the **Gazebo API → AWS** bridge |
| Fazheng | Post/expand the **security guardrails** research; threat model for ROS + WiFi |
| Sibgha | Research accessibility options; draft **user stories**; start **mobile app / UI** design; share case-study paper |
| All | Refine your area's points → consolidate in Google Slides → export PPT |

*Speaker notes:* Concrete, owned next steps so the panel sees momentum.

---

## Slide 13 — References & Resources

- Accessibility **case study / paper** (Sibgha — to be added).
- Security **guardrails research file** — in the Teams chat (Fazheng).
- Project network docs: NUwave connection, ROS 2 over NUwave (repo `docs/network/`).

*Speaker notes:* Fill in the paper link before Wednesday so this isn't a placeholder.

---

## Slide 14 — Closing / Q&A

- Robot is **off the bench and onto campus**; cloud path validated.
- Four workstreams advancing in parallel: connectivity, simulation, security, accessibility.
- Questions?

*Speaker notes:* End on the milestone + the four-track structure. Invite questions.

---

### Pre-Wednesday checklist
- [ ] Sibgha sends the accessibility case-study paper for the references slide
- [ ] Fazheng confirms the guardrails file link for references
- [ ] Everyone refines their own slides' bullets
- [ ] Kalhar builds the Google Slides deck from this file
- [ ] Team review pass → export to PPT
